summary: Back in the early 1950s, we had a big house in Champaign, Illinois, where I was teaching at the University of Illinois. My wife thought it was too expensive for us to keep, so she convinced me to build a smaller house and get a cabin in Colorado. We wanted our kids, who were about eight and ten years old then, to get out of Illinois during the summer.
cues: kids eight and ten years old

summary: I was teaching at the University of Illinois in the early 1950s when my wife suggested we build a cabin in Colorado for the kids to get out of Illinois in the summer. I retired from the University in 1969, as they automatically retired you at sixty-eight unless you were a special person, which I wasn't. My career there spanned about forty years before I moved to Colorado permanently.

Although my wife was originally from Pueblo, Colorado, she wanted to move to Colorado and talked me into building a cabin near Estes Park in 1951. I never liked Estes Park much because it was too touristy. We considered buying property there, but I ended up buying a two-acre lot laid out by Sig Warier nearby instead.

My wife was originally from Pueblo, Colorado, but she didn't want to go back there. Instead, she wanted to move to Colorado generally, which is why she convinced me to build a cabin near Estes Park. Pueblo was part of her background, but not the place she wanted to settle.

Sig Warier was the man who laid out the two-acre lots where we built our cabin near Estes Park in 1951. He had roads and lots planned before the state regulations changed. Sig also offered to arrange building the house if I drew the plans, and he promised it would be ready by the Fourth of July that year. Later, he had a spring that served several cabins, but eventually people had to dig their own wells.
cues: built house by Fourth of July

summary: When my wife died in 1972, I bought a cemetery lot in Longmont because she wanted to donate her body to medical science, but I preferred a burial site. We had also considered retiring in Longmont and even put a down payment on a house there in 1967, but the sale fell through. Longmont was a practical choice for us, though we ended up staying at our cabin near Estes Park.

In 1951, Sig Warier promised to have our cabin ready by the Fourth of July, and we arrived to find just a concrete foundation. We spent the entire summer building it with our kids' help and finally moved in by Labor Day. The Fourth of July marked the start of our summer project that year.

We moved into our cabin around Labor Day in 1951 after spending the summer constructing it. At that time, the water supply from Sig Warier's spring was shut off around Labor Day because it would freeze. Labor Day marked both our move-in and the seasonal water cutoff.

Memorial Day was the time when the water supply from the spring near our cabin was turned back on each year. Since the pipe was above ground and would freeze in winter, the water was only available from Memorial Day through Labor Day. This seasonal water cycle was a challenge until we dug our own well.

We installed an electric hot water heater from Sears Roebuck in our cabin. Also, my father, Walter Fitch, once acquired a high-wheeled automobile sold by Sears Roebuck, which was fixed up by a man who worked off his doctor bill. Sears Roebuck played a role in both our cabin utilities and family history.

I once heard about Robert Horner's book 'On Gay Street' from the 1920s, which my sister had. When I tried to buy a house on Gay Street in Longmont in 1967, the real estate dealer mentioned the book. It was a small connection that came up during our retirement house search.

My sister-in-law, Elisabeth S. Fitch, painted Meeker Park in 1961. She stayed at our cabin and tried to paint Meekers from there but had to go up to Meeker Park Lodge because of the trees. It was a memorable artistic visit with kids watching her paint.

Elisabeth S. Fitch, my sister-in-law and my wife's older sister, fancied herself as an artist. In 1961, she stayed at our cabin and painted Meeker Park, even setting up her easel at Meeker Park Lodge. She was part of the family and contributed art that we still have.

I once found an old book titled 'How to get to the Gold Fields of California' dated around 1850 or 1851 while cleaning out the University of Illinois library. It was interesting because it related to the California Gold Rush era, although I regret throwing the book away. The map inside showed areas before West Virginia existed, which came later during the Civil War.

West Virginia didn't exist on the map I found from around 1850; it was formed during the American Civil War about ten years later. The map showed Pennsylvania and Virginia before the Mason-Dixon line was agreed upon. This historical context helped me understand the changes in state boundaries over time.

The American Civil War, which happened about ten years after 1850, led to the creation of West Virginia. It also influenced the agreement on the Mason-Dixon line, which settled disputes over state boundaries. This war was a pivotal event in shaping the map I studied from the mid-19th century.

The Mason-Dixon line was the boundary that states agreed upon after some disputes when the states were first formed. It separated Pennsylvania from Virginia and was a significant factor in the historical maps I examined. The line also played a role in the Civil War era divisions.

Silver City in Arkansas was mentioned in relation to a stock certificate I found. Although I don't know if it ever had much gold, it did have coal. It was part of my father's various dealings, as he sometimes took goods in trade when people couldn't pay cash.

Walter Fitch was my father, and he often accepted various items in trade when people couldn't pay him. For example, he acquired a high-wheeled automobile sold by Sears Roebuck, which was fixed up by a man who worked off his doctor bill. My father's resourcefulness was part of our family history.
Boettcher & Company was not directly mentioned in detail in my story, so I don't have personal experiences or connections to share about them from this transcript.

As I mentioned, my wife was originally from Pueblo, Colorado, though she didn't want to return there. Pueblo was part of her roots but not where we decided to settle. Instead, we chose to build a cabin near Estes Park and eventually retire in Colorado.
New Mexico was mentioned briefly in the context of regional geography near Colorado, but I don't have any personal stories or connections related to New Mexico in this transcript.
